title: Strings Deep Dive
simple_definition: Strings are sequences of characters. Python has 40+ built-in string methods for manipulation.
technical_definition: Strings are immutable sequences of Unicode characters with rich method support and f-string formatting.
analogy: A string is like a necklace — each bead is a character, accessible by position.
syntax:
s = 'Hello World'
s.upper()       # HELLO WORLD
s.lower()       # hello world
s.split(' ')    # ['Hello', 'World']
s.replace('o','0')  # Hell0 W0rld
s.strip()       # remove whitespace
s[0:5]          # Hello
f'{s} Python'   # Hello World Python
example_desc: Practical examples demonstrating Strings Deep Dive.
code_example:
text = 'Python Programming'
print(text.upper())
print(text.lower())
print(text.title())
print(text.replace('Python', 'Java'))
print(text.find('Pro'))
print('Python' in text)
print(text.startswith('Py'))
words = text.split(' ')
print(words)
print('-'.join(words))
padded = '  hello  '
print(padded.strip())
name, age = 'Alice', 25
print(f'{name} is {age} years old')
print(text[:6])
print(text[::-1])
output: # Run the code to see output
logic_steps: Understand the core concept of Strings Deep Dive
Trace through the example step by step
edge_cases: Always test with edge cases: empty input, None, zero
Check for type errors when mixing data types
Consider what happens with very large or very small values
common_mistakes: Skipping the basics and jumping to complex code
Not testing code with different inputs
interview_tips: Strings Deep Dive is commonly tested in Python interviews
Understand the time and space complexity
Be ready to explain your reasoning

subtopics.title: String Basics & Indexing
subtopics.explanation: Strings are sequences of characters. Access individual characters with indexing. Slice to get substrings.
subtopics.syntax:
s = 'Python'
s[0]    # 'P' (first)
s[-1]   # 'n' (last)
s[1:4]  # 'yth' (slice)
s[::-1] # 'nohtyP' (reverse)
len(s)  # 6
subtopics.code:
s = 'Python Programming'
print(s[0])      # P
print(s[-1])     # g
print(s[7:18])   # Programming
print(s[:6])     # Python
print(s[::2])    # Pto rgamn
print(s[::-1])   # gnimmargorP nohtyP
print(len(s))    # 18

# Strings are immutable
# s[0] = 'J'  # TypeError!

# Check membership
print('Python' in s)   # True
print('Java' in s)     # False

# Iterate
for char in 'Hi!':
    print(char, end=' ')
subtopics.output:
P
g
Programming
Python
Po rgamn
gnimmargorP nohtyP
18
True
False
H i !
subtopics.tip: Remember: strings are immutable. You can't change a character — you create a new string.

subtopics.title: String Methods
subtopics.explanation: Python has 40+ built-in string methods. They all return new strings (strings are immutable).
subtopics.syntax:
s.upper()          # UPPERCASE
s.lower()          # lowercase
s.title()          # Title Case
s.strip()          # remove whitespace
s.split(',')       # split to list
','.join(lst)      # join list to string
s.replace(a, b)    # replace
s.find('x')        # index or -1
s.startswith('x')  # True/False
s.count('x')       # count occurrences
subtopics.code:
text = '  Hello, World!  '

print(text.strip())           # remove spaces
print(text.upper())
print(text.lower())
print(text.title())
print(text.replace('World', 'Python'))
print(text.find('World'))
print(text.count('l'))
print(text.startswith('  H'))
print(text.endswith('!  '))

# split and join
csv = 'Alice,Bob,Charlie,Dave'
names = csv.split(',')
print(names)
print(' | '.join(names))

# Check methods
print('hello123'.isalnum())
print('hello'.isalpha())
print('123'.isdigit())
print('  '.isspace())
subtopics.output:
Hello, World!
  HELLO, WORLD!  
  hello, world!  
  Hello, World!  
  Hello, Python!  
9
3
True
True
['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'Dave']
Alice | Bob | Charlie | Dave
True
True
True
True
subtopics.tip: String methods return NEW strings — they don't modify the original. Always assign the result: s = s.strip()

subtopics.title: f-strings & Formatting
subtopics.explanation: f-strings (Python 3.6+) are the modern way to embed variables in strings. Fast, readable, and powerful.
subtopics.syntax:
name = 'Alice'
age = 25

# f-string
print(f'Name: {name}, Age: {age}')

# Expressions inside {}
print(f'Next year: {age + 1}')

# Format numbers
pi = 3.14159
print(f'Pi = {pi:.2f}')  # 2 decimal places
print(f'{1000000:,}')    # 1,000,000
subtopics.code:
name = 'Alice'
age = 25
score = 98.5678
big_num = 1234567

# Basic f-string
print(f'Hello, {name}!')
print(f'{name} is {age} years old')

# Expressions
print(f'Next year: {age + 1}')
print(f'Upper: {name.upper()}')

# Number formatting
print(f'Score: {score:.2f}')    # 2 decimals
print(f'Score: {score:.0f}')    # no decimals
print(f'Big: {big_num:,}')      # comma separator
print(f'Hex: {255:#x}')         # hex
print(f'Padded: {name:>10}')    # right align
print(f'Padded: {name:<10}|')   # left align

# Debug (Python 3.8+)
x = 42
print(f'{x = }')  # x = 42
subtopics.output:
Hello, Alice!
Alice is 25 years old
Next year: 26
Upper: ALICE
Score: 98.57
Score: 99
Big: 1,234,567
Hex: 0xff
Padded:      Alice
Padded: Alice     |
x = 42
subtopics.tip: f-strings are 2x faster than .format() and much more readable. Always prefer f-strings in Python 3.6+.

problem_first:
Suppose you receive user data as text:
'  Alice,25,NYC  '

You need to:
1. Remove extra spaces
2. Split by comma
3. Get the name (first part)
4. Convert to uppercase

Without string methods, this would be very complex.
With Python strings:

data = '  Alice,25,NYC  '
data = data.strip()           # 'Alice,25,NYC'
parts = data.split(',')       # ['Alice', '25', 'NYC']
name = parts[0].upper()       # 'ALICE'

Python has 40+ built-in string methods for exactly this!
why_matters:
Strings are everywhere in programming:
🌐🌐 Web: parse URLs, HTML, JSON responses
📊 Data: clean and process CSV data
🔐 Security: validate passwords, sanitize input
🤖 NLP: text processing, sentiment analysis
📧 Email: parse and format messages

Mastering strings = mastering data processing.
important_points: ⭐ Strings are IMMUTABLE — you cannot change a character. s[0]='J' raises TypeError
⭐ String methods return NEW strings — they don't modify the original
⭐ Indexing starts at 0: s[0]=first, s[-1]=last, s[-2]=second from last
⭐ f-strings (Python 3.6+) are the fastest and most readable way to format strings
⭐ in operator checks substring: 'Python' in 'I love Python' → True
⭐ str.split() returns a list; str.join() combines a list into a string
⭐ strip() removes whitespace from both ends; lstrip()/rstrip() for one side

steps.action: String 'Hello' stored: H=0, e=1, l=2, l=3, o=4
